name: Emma-O
description: Emma-O, also known as Enma-Daio, is the King and Judge of Hell (Jigoku) in Japanese Buddhist mythology. He presides over the afterlife, determining the fate of souls based on their actions in life. Emma-O's origins trace back to Yama, the Hindu god of death, who was adopted and adapted into Japanese Buddhist beliefs. He is depicted as a stern and imposing figure, often with a large head, bulging eyes, and a full beard, reflecting his role as a rigorous judge. His powers include the ability to see into the past lives of individuals, discern truth from lies, and deliver just punishments. Emma-O commands a legion of oni (demons) who assist him in the tortures of hell. He is not inherently evil but rather a necessary force for maintaining cosmic balance, ensuring that karmic debts are repaid. He is often associated with scrolls showing the scenes of hell, and his image serves as a reminder of the consequences of immoral behavior. Prayers and offerings are sometimes made to Emma-O, not to escape punishment if deserving, but to plead for leniency and a swift judgment. Emma-O's judgment is often influenced by the ten kings who also preside over portions of hell, each specializing in a particular aspect of judgment and punishment. The length of stay and type of torture suffered in hell depend on the severity of their sins, before reincarnation. Emma-O's role is pivotal in the cycle of death and rebirth, guiding souls through the afterlife and ensuring justice prevails, ultimately playing a significant role in shaping moral behavior in the mortal world.
subtitle: King and Judge of Hell

keyMyths.title: Judgment of Souls
keyMyths.description: Emma-O judges newly deceased souls, reviewing their past lives to determine their fate in the afterlife. He uses the Mirror of Truth to reveal their hidden actions and intentions.

keyMyths.title: The Ten Kings
keyMyths.description: Emma-O is assisted by ten other kings who preside over different areas of hell, each responsible for specific punishments and torments based on the sins committed.

keyMyths.title: Appeals to Emma-O
keyMyths.description: Some believe that prayers and offerings can be made to Emma-O to plead for leniency or a faster judgment, though true justice is always served according to karmic debt.

sources.author: Addison, Marc
sources.work: Japanese Ghosts and Demons: Art of the Supernatural
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of various Japanese supernatural beings, including Emma-O.
